title: "Azure Relic Puzzle v10799"

description: |
  At 20:55 the Azure Relic vanished from a locked alcove in the north gallery.
  Four badge-holders (1, 2, 3, 4) were recorded nearby.
  Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three are perfectly truthful.
  Exactly one suspect is guilty.

  Suspect 1:
    (1) 'If $L2$ then $\lnot G3$.'
    (2) 'If $T3$ then $\lnot P4$.'
    (3) '$P2 \oplus L4$.'

  Suspect 2:
    (1) '$P1$.'
    (2) 'If $L3$ then $T4$.'
    (3) '$G1 \oplus L4$.'

  Suspect 3:
    (1) '$\lnot P3$.'
    (2) 'If $T4$ then $P2$.'
    (3) '$L2 \oplus \lnot P1$.'

  Suspect 4:
    (1) '$P3$.'
    (2) 'If $L1$ then $P1$.'
    (3) '$\lnot G4$.'

  Notation:
    $Lx$: x lies in every one of their statements.
    $Tx$: x is entirely truthful.
    $Gx$: x is guilty.
    $Px$: x was present at 20:50.
    $\oplus$: exclusive-or.

question: "Who stole the Azure Relic? Return a single digit (1–4)."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume in turn that each suspect is the sole liar.

    Case L1: 1(1) makes L2 true, giving two liars – contradiction.

    Case L2: 2 is the only liar. From 2(1) false ⇒ $P1$ true.  
      2(3) false ⇒ $G1 \oplus L4$ false ⇒ $G1$ false and $L4$ false.  
      Truthful 1(3), 3(1), 4(3) rule out $G1$, $G3$, $G4$, so $G2$ must hold. No contradiction.

    Case L3: 3 liar with truthful 4 forces $P2$ true via 3(2), but 2(3) would then be true, clashing with L2 status – contradiction.

    Case L4: 4 liar implies $P3$ false by 4(1), conflicting with truthful 1(3) or 3(1) – contradiction.

    Only the L2 scenario remains consistent, giving G2.

  final: '2'
